It is a genus of flowering plants native to Asia, and its classification is part of the broader Lauraceae family. The Lauraceae family is further classified under the order Laurales and division Magnoliophyta. This classification is based on the plant's physical characteristics and genetic makeup. By classifying Synaedrys densiflora within the Lauraceae family, scientists can better understand its evolutionary history and relationships with other plant species.

Synaedrys densiflora, now known as Hamamelis, is a type of flowering plant that belongs to the family Hamamelidaceae. The plant has been traditionally used for its medicinal properties, including its ability to promote wound healing, reduce inflammation, and exhibit antimicrobial activity. The bioactive constituents of Hamamelis include phenolic compounds, flavonoids, and saponins, which are responsible for its medicinal effects. The most common herbal preparations made with Hamamelis include topical creams, ointments, and infusions, which are used to treat skin conditions such as eczema and wounds, as well as to soothe sore throats and coughs.
